coredata.dc:title: A single-cell analysis platform for electrochemiluminescent detection of platelets adhesion to endothelial cells based on Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Dongping
Shang, Yunfei
Qiu, Youyi
Zhou, Bin
Yang, Peihui
coredata.dc:description: A novel single-cell analysis platform (SCA) was developed for the investigation of platelets adhesion to single human umbilical vein endothelial cell (HUVEC) via using the adhesion molecule (E-selectin) on the damaged HUVEC as the marker site, and integrating electrochemiluminescence (ECL) with the ultrasensitive Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes. The Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanocomposite, a kind of double layer zinc-coadsorbed carbon quantum dot (ZnCQDs) core-shell nanoprobe, was firstly constructed by using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as the core to load with ZnCQDs and then the citrate-modified silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) as the bridge to link AuNPs-ZnCQDs with ZnCQDs to form the core-shell with double layer ZnCQDs (DL-ZnCQDs) nanoprobe, revealed a 10-fold signal amplification. The H2O2-induced oxidative damage HUVECs were utilized as the cellular model on which anti-E-selectin functionalized nanoprobes specially recognized E-selectin, the SCA showed that the ECL signals decreased with platelets adhesion to single HUVEC. The proposed SCA could effectively and dynamically monitor the adhesion between single HUVEC and platelets in the absence and presence of collagen activation, moreover, be able to quantitatively detect the number of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC, and show a good analytical performance with linear range from 1 to 15 platelets. In contrast, the HUVEC was down-regulated the expression of adhesion molecules by treating with quercetin inhibitor, and the SCA also exhibited the feasibility for analysis of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC. Therefore, the single-cell analysis platform provided a novel and promising protocol for analysis of the single intercellular adhesion, and it will be beneficial to elucidate the pathogenesis of cardiovascular diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Single-cell analysis
Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes
Platelets adhesion to endothelial cells
E-selectin

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes and modification with anti-E-selectin
HUVECs culture and H2O2-induced HUVECs injury
Assembly and characterization of Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes
ECL behaviors of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC
Dynamic monitoring of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC
Inhibition on platelets activation by drugs
Inhibition E-selectin expression on endothelial cells by quercetin
Evaluation of heterogeneity of platelets adhesion to single endothelial cell

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) curves of (A) AuNPs, zinc(II) ion and AgNPs. Inset was the amplified DPV curves of AgNPs. (B) DPV curves of layer-by-layer assembling the Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes. Inset was the amplified DPV curves of AgNPs on AuNPs-ZnCQDs-AgNPs and Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes. (C) ECL intensity-potential curves of layer-by-layer assembling the Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes. (D) The nanoprobes ECL stability under continuous scaning for 8 cycles in 0.1M PBS (pH 7.4) containing 0.1M KCl and 0.1M K2S2O8. The PMT voltage was set at 600V.

ECL intensity-potential curves for unactivated platelets (A) and activated platelets (C) adhesion to single damaged HUVEC. ECL intensity-time curves for unactivated platelets (B) and activated platelets (D) adhesion to single damaged HUVEC with five assays in parallel.

(A) ECL intensity-time curves of activated platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC in incubation time of: 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35min. The inset was the relationship curve of ECL intensity-time. (B) ECL intensity-time curves for the numbers of platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC: 0, 1,3, 5, 7,9,11,13,15,16,17,18 platelets. The inset is the corresponding linear relationship curve in a PBS buffer solution (0.1M, pH 7.4) containing 0.1M KCl and 0.1M K2S2O8. The PMT voltage was set at 750V, Error bars = RSD (n = 5).

(A) Histograms of ECL intensity of adhesion between single damaged HUVEC and platelets in treating with different drugs: (a) single damaged HUVEC; (b) unactivated platelets; (c) activated platelets; (d) activated platelets treating with 5mgmL−1 heparin; (d) activated platelets treating with 10mmolL−1 aspirin; (e) activated platelets treating with 25 μmol L−1 quercetin. (B) Histograms of ECL intensity in the absence (a) and presence (b) of quercetin treating with single damaged HUVEC. **p < 0.05, compared with the group c. Error bars = RSD (n = 5).
Histograms of ECL intensity of unactived and activated platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC. **p < 0.05, compared with similar cells.
The schematic of single-cell analysis platform for detection of platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC.

head.ce:title: A single-cell analysis platform for electrochemiluminescent detection of platelets adhesion to endothelial cells based on Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Jinan University, Guangzhou 510632, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Environmental Engineering, Zhuhai Campus, Beijing Institute of Technology, Zhuhai 519088, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Environmental Engineering, Zhuhai Campus, Beijing Institute of Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel single-cell analysis platform (SCA) was developed for the investigation of platelets adhesion to single human umbilical vein endothelial cell (HUVEC) via using the adhesion molecule (E-selectin) on the damaged HUVEC as the marker site, and integrating electrochemiluminescence (ECL) with the ultrasensitive Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes. The Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanocomposite, a kind of double layer zinc-coadsorbed carbon quantum dot (ZnCQDs) core-shell nanoprobe, was firstly constructed by using gold nanoparticles (AuNPs) as the core to load with ZnCQDs and then the citrate-modified silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) as the bridge to link AuNPs-ZnCQDs with ZnCQDs to form the core-shell with double layer ZnCQDs (DL-ZnCQDs) nanoprobe, revealed a 10-fold signal amplification. The H2O2-induced oxidative damage HUVECs were utilized as the cellular model on which anti-E-selectin functionalized nanoprobes specially recognized E-selectin, the SCA showed that the ECL signals decreased with platelets adhesion to single HUVEC. The proposed SCA could effectively and dynamically monitor the adhesion between single HUVEC and platelets in the absence and presence of collagen activation, moreover, be able to quantitatively detect the number of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC, and show a good analytical performance with linear range from 1 to 15 platelets. In contrast, the HUVEC was down-regulated the expression of adhesion molecules by treating with quercetin inhibitor, and the SCA also exhibited the feasibility for analysis of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC. Therefore, the single-cell analysis platform provided a novel and promising protocol for analysis of the single intercellular adhesion, and it will be beneficial to elucidate the pathogenesis of cardiovascular diseases.

ce:section.ce:para: Adhesion of platelets to endothelial cells played a pivotal role in cardiovascular disease, especially in the early steps of atherosclerosis (Tull et al., 2006). Endothelial cells were damaged by cytokines (Zapolska-Downar et al., 2004) and oxidative stress (Ugusman et al., 2011) with up-regulated the expression of adhesion molecules, such as endothelial-leukocyte adhesion molecule-1 (E-selectin), intracellular adhesion molecule-1 (ICAM-1) and vascular cell adhesion molecule-1 (VCAM-1), which caused leukocytes, platelets and endothelial cells adhesion and ultimately promoted atherosclerotic formation (Yau et al., 2015). Platelets, originated from megakaryocytes and circulated in blood as monitors of vascular integrity, were small anucleated blood cells and arrested hemorrhage after tissue trauma (Ruggeri and Mendolicchio, 2007), which were stimulated by collagen (Mizurini et al., 2013) leading to excessive expression of adhesion molecules and alterations in their shape, adhesion, aggregation, and subsequently thrombus formation (Li et al., 2017). Only when the endothelial cells were damaged by oxidative stress or pathological alterations, the adhesive potential of platelets to endothelial cells became firm, such as found in atherosclerosis (Varga-Szabo et al., 2008). Therefore, the investigation of platelets adhesion to oxidative damage endothelial cells, is of great significance to understand the pathogenesis of cardiovascular diseases, especially at the single-cell levels, due to their heterogeneity among cell populations.

Electrochemiluminescence, as a powerful analytical tool, integrates the advantages of electrochemistry and chemiluminescence, so it has been widely used in the field of bioanalysis, such as genosensors (Wang et al., 2015), immunoassay (Zhu et al., 2016) and cytosensors (Qiu et al., 2016), etc. Recently, the integrating of single-cell analysis with ECL technique, had attracted considerable attention in analysis of the intra- and extracellular biomolecules of the single cell, meanwhile, the topic usually suffered from the challenges resulting from the low expression levels of biomolecules and the small size at single-cell levels (Galler et al., 2014). Currently, only a few references have reported the analysis of biomolecules at single-cell levels by using ECL. Chen's group designed a cell-sized microwell microelectrode array (Tian et al., 2014) or a pinhole indium/tin oxide electrode (Chen et al., 2016) via luminol ECL to detect membrane cholesterol and sphingomyelin at single cell. The ECL imaging with luminol and a charge-coupled device was developed for analysis of glucose (Xu et al., 2016), membrane cholesterol (Xu et al., 2017) at single living cell. In our previous work, the single-cell analysis platform was constructed for detection of breast cancer cells and evaluation of the CD44 expression level based on zinc-coadsorbed carbon quantum dot ECL nanoprobe (Qiu et al., 2017a). Although, the as-reported single-cell analysis exhibits excellent ideas for fabrication of biosensing platform, it's still a challenge to analyze the intra- and extracellular biomolecules at the single-cell levels and needs to extend the application in research on their heterogeneity among cell populations. Up to now, there was no report concerning the research on the topic of platelets adhesion to endothelial cells at single-cell levels. The cellular adhesion played a critical role during various pathological and physiological processes. To elucidate the interaction between platelets and endothelial cells, many studies (Szuwart et al., 2000; Tanahashi et al., 2001) have been performed. The biological techniques of labeling platelets such as radiolabeling and counting were applied in the study of platelets adhesion to endothelial cells, however, these methods did not obtain full approval (Xu et al., 2010). Therefore, there's an urgent need to develop a simple, rapid, and highly sensitive assay for platelets-endothelial cells adhesion, especially at the single-cell levels, it will be beneficial to understand the pathogenesis of cardiovascular diseases.

It's well-known that a high-efficiency signal amplification strategy was the critical process in designing of the highly sensitive biosensing platform, especially for the single-cell analysis. The nanomaterials, such as magnetic beads (MBs) (Tang et al., 2015), graphene (Yang et al., 2013), and metal nanoparticles (Ahnad et al., 2015), as the amplification element were applied as an alternative carrier for enhancing the signal intensity by the multiple amplification strategy. The nanoprobes, such as carbon quantum dots (CQDs) (Xiong et al., 2016), semiconductor quantum dots (e.g., CdSe, CdTe) (Yang et al., 2017), due to their unique optical properties such as high quantum yields, excellent photostability and small size, have been widely used as the optics and ECL probes for biolabeling and bioanalysis. Recently, the multiple amplification strategy of nanoprobes demonstrated a series of nanocomposite probes and provided the excellent analytical performance. The Au/SiO2 core–shell nanoparticles was prepared to load with CQDs to achieve 8-fold enhancement of ECL signals (Zhang et al., 2015). The multiple amplification strategies based on AuNPs and MBs as carrier were designed for improving the ECL intensity of nanoprobes with enhancement of 8-fold for CQDs and 16-fold for luminol respectively, to analyze multiple latent tuberculosis infection markers (Zhou et al., 2017). Tang et al. utilized CdTe and CdSe QDs to fabricate CdTe/CdSe QDs-based paper fluorescence sensor (Qiu et al., 2017b; Lin et al., 2017) and CdTe QDs-mediated hole-trapping photoelectrochemistry analytical device (Lin et al., 2016) for highly sensitive detection of biomolecules. In addition, Wu et al. prepared the ECL nanocomposite probes based Ru(bpy)3 2+ which was combined to Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Li et al., 2017a) or 3D CuNi oxalate nanomaterials (Li et al., 2017b) for highly sensitive immunoassay (Han et al., 2018). In this work, the Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes was firstly constructed by using AuNPs as the core to load with ZnCQDs and then the citrate-modified AgNPs as the bridge via zinc(II) ion combining to link AuNPs-ZnCQDs with ZnCQDs to form the core-shell with double layer ZnCQDs (DL-ZnCQDs) nanoprobes, which could be used as the ultrasensitive ECL probes for the single-cell analysis.

Herein, the single-cell analysis platform was fabricated for the first time to research on platelets adhesion to single endothelial cell by using the E-selectin on the damaged HUVEC as the marker site, and integrating ECL with the sensitive Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes (as shown in Scheme 1). Single HUVEC was in-situ incubated on the indium tin oxide (ITO) electrode and treated with H2O2 as the cellular model on which the expression of E-selectin was upregulated under oxidative stress, and anti-E-selectin functionalized nanoprobes could be used to recognize E-selectin. As the platelets were activated by collagen stimuli, the adhesive potential of platelets to the damaged endothelial cells became firm resulting in the decrease of ECL signal, thus, it could be used as the single-cell analysis platform to quantitative detection the number of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC. Futhermore, the SCA was further applied to the studies of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC under the drugs stimuli, such as the inhibitors of aspirin, heparin and quercetin for platelets and quercetin for HUVEC. Therefore, the proposed strategy not only provided a novel and effective single-cell analysis platform for analysis of the single intercellular adhesion, but also be able to extend the applications in the drug analysis.

Potassium peroxodisulfate, zinc acetate, and poly(ethylene glycol) (PEG; Mr = 4000) were purchased from Big Alum Chemical Reagent Factory (Tianjin, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were obtained from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent (Guangzhou,China). Heparin sodium, and quercetin were obtained from Feibo Biological Technology Co. Ltd (Guangzhou, China). HAuCl4·4H2O, silver nitrate (AgNO3) and collagen protein was purchased from Guangzhou Qiyun Biotechnology Co. Ltd (Guangzhou,China). Aspirin was obtained from Xinhui Pharmaceutical Co Ltd (HuNan, China). The HUVECs were donated by the University of Jinan School of Medicine. All aqueous solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (> 18MΩ, Mill-Q Millpore).

The ECL measurements were performed with a model MPI-E from ECL analytical system (Xi’an, China,) using a conventional three electrode system. The three-electrode system was comprised of a platinum wire as the auxiliary electrode, Ag/AgCl (saturated KCl) as the reference electrode and ITO electrodes as working electrode. Differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) was performed with a CHI600E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co.). The morphology of the nanoprobes were visually characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM; Philips). The atomic force microscopy (AFM) system was BioScope Catalyst (Bruker, German). The cell imaging was characterized by BM-37XB inverted microscopen (Shanghai, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes and modification with anti-E-selectin
ce:section.ce:para: The Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were prepared as follows: ZnCQDs was prepared according to a published procedure (Qiu et al., 2017a). 10mL of ZnCQDs was mixted with 1.0mL of 10−4 M freshly prepared AuNPs under vigorous stirring for 1h and centrifuged (10000rpm, 10min) to obtain AuNPs-ZnCQDs and then diluted with water 10mL; 0.5mL of 10−3 M AgNPs was added to as-prepared AuNPs-ZnCQDs under ultrasonication for 30min and centrifuged (10000rpm, 10min) to obtain AuNPs-ZnCQDs-AgNPs nanocomposites. Subsequently, 2mL of the AuNPs-ZnCQDs-AgNPs nanocomposites was mixted with 10mL of ZnCQDs under ultrasonication for 30min and centrifuged. The Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were redispersed in 5mL of a PBS buffer solution.

The anti-E-selectin modified Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were prepared by adding 2.5mL of the as-proposed Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes to 100μL of 50μgmL−1 anti-E-selectin mixture containing 1-ethyl-3-[3-(dimethylamino)propyl]carbodiimide (EDC; 75mM) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS; 15mM), and this mixture was vibrated softly for 1h at room temperature. To remove excess anti-E-selectin, NHS, and EDC, the mixed solution was centrifuged and then washed several times with a PBS solution. Finally, the anti-E-selectin functionalized Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were stored at 4°C for the following experiments. The optimization of preparation conditions of Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes was presented in Fig. S1.

Venous bloods were donated by the First Affiliated Hospital of Jinan University and collected with K3EDTA anticoagulant tubes as anticoagulant (Li et al., 2017). Platelet rich plasma (PRP) was extracted by centrifugation at 800rpm for 10min, added to 1mL PBS solution (pH 7.4) and centrifuged at 3000rpm for 10min. The supernatant was discarded and the platelets were suspended in PBS buffer solution (pH 7.4). Then, Platelets were stimulated by 20mgmL−1 collagen for 15min and then centrifuged at 3000rpm for 8min to suspend in PBS solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: HUVECs culture and H2O2-induced HUVECs injury
ce:section.ce:para: HUVECs were cultured in an RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, 100 UmL−1 streptomycin, and penicillin and maintained at 37°C under a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2. Those cells were selected for the following experiments at the logarithmic phase.

The ITO electrode was sonicated in acetone, ethanol and ultrapure water for 15min and dried at room temperature. Then, ITO electrode was sterilized in an autoclaves and placed in clean benches. HUVECs were collected and separated from the DMEM medium through centrifugation at 800rpm for 5min. These cells were then resuspended with PBS solution (0.1M, pH 7.4) of 100 cell mL−1. Single HUVEC was captured on the ITO electrode via the pipette and was placed into 12-well plates and incubated at 37°C in a humidified atmosphere containing 5% CO2 for 12h, treating of HUVECs with 400 umol L−1 H2O2 for 6h for the following experiments.

Anti-E-selectin functionalized Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were used to label the E-selectin on H2O2-induced oxidative damage HUVEC for 60min, followed by washing carefully with PBS solution (0.1M, pH 7.4) to remove the excessive ECL nanoprobe. Unactivated and activated platelets were incubated with single damaged HUVEC for 30min, and to remove the nano-adhesion platelets, the ITO electrode was slowly washed with PBS. The platelets-incubated single damaged HUVEC on ITO electrode was immersed in PBS (0.1M, pH 7.4) containing K2S2O8 (0.1M) and the potential scanned from 0 to −2.0V, and the photomultiplier tube (PMT) was set at 750V. Then, the ECL signals were recorded in PBS (0.1M, pH 7.4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assembly and characterization of Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes
ce:section.ce:para: The Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were constructed by using AuNPs as the core to load with ZnCQDs and then citrate-modified AgNPs as the bridge via zinc(II) ion combining to link AuNPs-ZnCQDs with ZnCQDs to form the core-shell DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobe. Electrochemistry and TEM were employed to characterize the layer-by-layer assembly process of the nanoprobes. As shown in Fig. 1, the oxidation peaks of alone zinc(II) ion, and AgNPs appeared at −0.89V and −0.06V with the potential scanning from −1.20V to + 0.60V (Fig. 1A), and that of ZnCQDs appeared at −1.25V for zinc(II) and −1.58V for CQDs with the potential of −2.00V to + 0.60V, respectively (Fig. 1B). For the Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes, it exhibited three oxidation peaks (−0.10V, −1.33V and −1.62V) corresponding to the oxidation of AgNPs, zinc(II) ion and CQDs with the peak potentials shifting in negative direction, respectively (Fig. 1B), which confirmed AgNPs as the bridge via zinc(II) ion combining to link AuNPs-ZnCQDs with ZnCQDs to form the DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobe, so the Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were prepared successfully. TEM imaging was further applied to verify the layer-by-layer assembly process of the Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes. As shown in Fig. S2, the average diameter of the ZnCQDs was ∼18.6nm, and the first layer of ZnCQDs attached on AuNPs (~50nm) to form Au-ZnCQDs with the size of ∼70nm. The AgNPs were embed in the layer of ZnCQDs, that was used as a bridge to link Au-ZnCQDs with ZnCQDs to obtain Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes with the size of ~100nm. Therefore, TEM images further confirmed the successfully assembling of Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes.

ECL behaviors of Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were characterized in PBS (0.1M, pH 7.4) with K2S2O8 (0.1M) as the coreactant (Fig. 1C,D). The multiple signal amplifications of the Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were confirmed, in which ECL intensity for AuNPs-ZnCQDs had an enhancement of 4.5 times, due to loading amount of ZnCQDs on AuNPs, and citrate-modified AgNPs were successively used as a bridge via zinc(II) ion combining to link AuNPs-ZnCQDs with ZnCQDs to form Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes with the signal enhancing of 6.7 and 10 times corresponding to single layer and double layer of ZnCQDs, respectively (Fig. 1C). While the ECL nanoprobes were determined continuously by 8 cycles of potential scanning, the data showed that the ECL nanoprobes had excellent stability (Fig. 1D). These results demonstrated that the ultrasensitive Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes had the potential applications in the following single-cell adhesion analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: ECL behaviors of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC
ce:section.ce:para: ECL behaviors of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC were studied by using E-selectin as a marker site to recognize anti-E-selectin functionalized nanoprobes. Single HUVEC was incubated on the ITO electrode for 12h and then treated of HUVEC with 400 μmol L−1 H2O2 for 6h as the cellular model. The inverted microscope images of the unactivated and activated platelets was shown in Fig. S3. The unactivated platelets had a good dispersion (Fig. S3A), while collagen-activated platelets aggregated around as the particles deposit on substrate (Fig. S3B). In Fig. 2A,C, the typical ECL curves were obtained by detection of the activated platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC as the unactivated platelets as the control, and both of ECL signals declined, but the activated platelets decreased more obviously, owing to excessive expression of adhesion molecules on collagen-stimulated platelets. Fig. 2B,D the ECL-time curves for platelets adhesion to single HUVEC were measured in parallel with five samples, and exhibited a good reproducibility for both of unactivated and activated platelets, but the latter revealed the decreasing ECL signals due to the strong adhesion potential of activated platelets. The results showed that the single-cell analysis platform could effectively detect the adhesion interaction of platelets and HUVEC. Inverted microscopy (Fig. S4) and atomic force microscopy (Fig. S5) were used to further characterize the morphology of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC, which revealed more numbers of activated platelets adhesion to single HUVEC than the control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dynamic monitoring of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC
ce:section.ce:para: Because of the time-dependent relationship of platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC, the SCA was applied to dynamically monitoring platelets adhesion. In Fig. 3A, it showed that the incubation time of activated platelets had a significant effect on ECL signal, and the ECL intensity decreased with the adhesion time increasing, and then tended to constant in 20min. It implied that the number of platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC surface tended to saturation. Therefore, the single-cell analysis platform was capable of evaluation the adhesive potential between platelets and single damaged HUVEC.

The single-cell analysis platform was applied to quantitative detection the number of platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC by using anti-E-selectin functionalized nanoprobes to label E-selectin on HUVECs. As shown in Fig. 3B, the ECL intensity was proportional to the number of platelets ranging from 1 to 15 platelets, but the ECL signal deviated from a linear relationship as the number over 15 due to the platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC surface tending to saturate, and the average adhesion number of single damaged HUVEC was about 15 ± 2 platelets. The linear regression equation was ΔIECL = 98.5N − 30.1 with the correlation coefficient of 0.9878 (n = 5), where △IECL represented the difference of ECL signal between single damaged HUVEC and activated platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC, N was the platelets number and n was the number of repeated measurements, respectively. To verify the feasibility of the proposed method, inverted microscope was employed to observe the number of platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC, and the results were in good agreement with that of by the SCA (Table S1). These results clearly demonstrated that the single-cell analysis platform could effectively and sensitively detect the number of platelets adhesion to single HUVEC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition on platelets activation by drugs
ce:section.ce:para: It's reported that aspirin and heparin had a significant inhibitory effect on collagen-induced platelets aggregation (Floyd et al., 2014) and reduced platelets adhesion to HUVECs (Li et al., 2007). Quercetin was one of the most prominent dietary antioxidants that had the ability to remove free radicals, antioxidant, and antiplatelet aggregation (Kashyap et al., 2016). So, the aspirin, heparin, and quercetin were used as the inhibitors to suppress the collagen-induced platelets activation, and the SCA was applied to detect the platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC under the stimulation of several drugs. Compared with the collagen-activated platelets, the platelets were stimulated by the aspirin and heparin inhibitors, and then the adhesive potential was obviously decreased that of unactivated platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC, but quercetin had less inhibitory effect on platelets than the other two drugs ( Fig. 4A). The drugs inhibited the platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC as shown in Table S2. Thus, this single-cell analysis platform was used to research the inhibitory effect of drugs on activated platelets, and it will be extended to study the pathogenesis of cardiovascular diseases and drugs analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Inhibition E-selectin expression on endothelial cells by quercetin
ce:section.ce:para: Quercetin was utilized to down-regulate the expression levels of E-selectin on HUVECs (Yang et al., 2015), and the SCA was applied to examine the platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC. As shown in Fig. 4B, the ECL intensity was remarkably decreased in comparision with the control, indicating that quercetin could inhibite the expression of E-selectin on HUVEC and resulting in the reduction of labeling nanoprobes. The result suggested that quercetin could protect HUVECs from oxidative stress by decreasing expression of adhesion molecules and inhibit platelets adhesion to HUVECs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of heterogeneity of platelets adhesion to single endothelial cell
ce:section.ce:para:
To evaluate the heterogeneity of the adhesion between single damaged HUVEC and platelets in the absence and presence of collagen activation, respectively, a total of 40 single damaged HUVEC were used for statistical analysis by the SCA. As shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. S6, the average of ΔIECL value for unactivated platelets-HUVEC adhesion was 686.9 ± 88.4a.u. (
x
¯
± s, n = 40) with a coefficient of variation (CV) of 12.9%, revealing data with larger variation. Therefore, a CV of 5% was chosen as the reference level for evaluation of heterogeneity of platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC. The statistical result indicated that the data of 19 single damaged HUVEC was 673.7 ± 32.1a.u. (CV ≤ 5%), indicating a similar of platelets adhesive potential to single damaged HUVEC. The data of the other 21 single damaged HUVEC was calculated to be 699.3 ± 118.7a.u. (CV > 5%), revealing a different adhesion ability of platelets to single damaged HUVEC. For activated platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC, the ΔIECL value was counted with 1145.9 ± 132.3a.u. (
x
¯
± s, n = 40) with a CV of 11.5%. The data value of 18 single damaged HUVEC was 1117.2 ± 69.4a.u. (CV ≤ 5%), implying a similar adhesion ability of platelets to single damaged HUVEC. The data of the other 22 single damaged HUVEC was 1170.5 ± 166.2a.u. (CV > 5%), indicating data with larger volatility. Thus, the above data respectively revealed the heterogeneity of single cellular adhesion due to the difference of cellular biological function. It could be concluded that the single-cell analysis platform could effectively and sensitively to analyze the single intercellular interaction.

In summary, the single-cell analysis platform was developed for the first time to research on platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC via using the E-selectin on HUVEC as the marker site, and integrating ECL ultrasensitive Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes. The Au@DL-ZnCQDs nanoprobes were firstly constructed by using AuNPs as the core to load with ZnCQDs and then the AgNPs as the bridge to link AuNPs-ZnCQDs with ZnCQDs to obtain the core-shell with double layer ZnCQDs nanoprobe, exhibited a 10-fold signal amplification. The H2O2-induced oxidative damage HUVECs were selected as the cellular model by anti-E-selectin functionalized nanoprobes to recognize E-selectin on HUVEC. The SCA could effectively and dynamically monitor the platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC under the stimulation of several drugs. In addition, the SCA exhibited a good analytical performance for quantitative detection of the number of platelets adhesion to single damaged HUVEC ranging from 1 to 15 platelets. Therefore, the proposed-SCA provided a novel protocol to analyze the single intercellular adhesion, and it will be helpful to understand the pathogenesis of cardiovascular diseases and be able to extend the applications in the drug analysis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financed by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21375048).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bios.2017.11.058.
